text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

Patient: Robert Delacroix
MRN: [REDACTED]
Clinical History: 70-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, germline BRCA2 mutation. Currently on Xtandi (approximately 4 months) plus ongoing ADT (Lupron). Prior therapy: abi/prednisone discontinued at CRPC transition. Known osseous metastases (lumbar spine L2-L4, pelvis, right femur). Known pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator, left external iliac) at prior staging. Evaluation for treatment response.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from approximately 22 months prior (initial staging).

Technique: Multi-detector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed following intravenous administration of iohexol 120 mL at 2 mL/s. Portal venous phase images of the abdomen and pelvis were obtained. Axial, coronal, and sagittal multiplanar reconstructions reviewed. Dose modulation employed. No prior allergic reaction to contrast documented.

---

FINDINGS

CHEST:

Lungs and pleura: The lungs are clear. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules identified. The visualized airways are patent. No pneumothorax.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The heart is mildly enlarged in configuration, without pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels appear normal in caliber.

Chest wall and soft tissues: Osseous structures of the thorax are without lytic or new suspicious lesions. Mild thoracic spondylosis noted. No rib lesions identified.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal and hepatic veins are patent on this phase.

Gallbladder and biliary system: Gallbladder is present without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct within normal limits.

Pancreas: Normal size and parenchymal attenuation throughout. No ductal dilatation or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Mildly enlarged at 13.4 cm in craniocaudal dimension, without focal lesions. This finding is nonspecific and may reflect prior or ongoing treatment effect.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are within normal limits in size and attenuation. No adrenal nodules or masses identified bilaterally.

Kidneys: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically without hydronephrosis or obstructive uropathy. No renal masses. A 5 mm simple cortical cyst in the right kidney is unchanged and benign.

Gastrointestinal tract: Small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness. Colon is well-distended and unremarkable. Appendix not visualized in its entirety but there is no periappendiceal inflammation.

Peritoneum and retroperitoneum: No ascites. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Aorta is of normal caliber throughout.

PELVIS:

Prostate and seminal vesicles: The prostate gland is enlarged and heterogeneous, consistent with known primary malignancy. No significant change in overall gland configuration compared to prior. Bilateral seminal vesicle involvement appears similar to prior.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is well-distended and without filling defects or wall thickening.

Pelvic lymph nodes:
- Right obturator lymph node: now measures 1.3 cm in short axis (previously 2.1 cm on prior CT). This represents interval decrease.
- Left external iliac lymph node: now measures 0.9 cm in short axis (previously 1.4 cm). Also decreased in size.
- No new pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes meeting criteria for enlargement.
- No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Osseous structures (pelvis and visualized spine):
The previously described sclerotic and mixed lytic-sclerotic lesions in the lumbar vertebral bodies (L2, L3, L4), right iliac wing, sacroiliac joint region, and right femoral neck are again visualized. Overall, the bony lesions appear stable to slightly more sclerotic compared to prior imaging. There is no appreciable new lytic destruction or cortical breakthrough. Sclerosis of previously mixed lytic-sclerotic foci is a recognized pattern of treatment response in osseous metastatic disease.

No new suspicious osseous lesions are identified on this CT.

Soft tissues of the pelvis are without suspicious masses or free fluid.

---

IMPRESSION

1. Interval decrease in size of the right obturator lymph node (2.1 cm to 1.3 cm) and left external iliac lymph node (1.4 cm to 0.9 cm) compared to prior staging CT, consistent with treatment response in the setting of Xtandi therapy.

2. No new pelvic, retroperitoneal, or mediastinal/hilar lymphadenopathy identified.

3. Known osseous metastatic disease in the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur appears stable, with increased sclerosis in some previously mixed lytic-sclerotic lesions - a pattern compatible with ongoing treatment effect in bone. No new bony lesions detected on CT.

4. No hepatic lesions, pulmonary nodules, or adrenal abnormalities. No evidence of visceral metastatic disease.

5. Mild splenomegaly, nonspecific in this clinical context.

Findings reviewed with the referring oncology team.

Signing Radiologist: [REDACTED], MD
Department of Radiology, Body Imaging Section
